entry.title: Question regarding IAS Group Filtering and Authorization Mapping
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We have a question regarding the runtime processing of group attributes in SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS).

In our landscape, users are authenticated by Microsoft Entra ID, and many users belong to a large number of Entra groups that are used for different enterprise applications.

For some SAP applications, authorization is evaluated locally after authentication. However, for other SAP applications, authorization is based on mapping Microsoft Entra groups to application roles or SAP role collections. In these scenarios, the groups received in the SAML assertion (or other supported token) determine the user's access within the application.

Our question is:

If Microsoft Entra ID sends a multi-valued groups claim containing many group memberships (for example, 50 groups), can SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) filter or transform this attribute on a per-application basis so that only the groups relevant to the target application are included in the outgoing SAML assertion?

For example:

  • Microsoft Entra user belongs to 50 groups.
  • Signavio requires only 2 of those groups.
  • SAP Build Work Zone requires a different subset.
  • Another SAP application requires yet another subset.

Can IAS be configured so that each application receives only its relevant groups, without exposing unrelated Entra groups?

We are asking this because we would like IAS to act as the central identity and authorization abstraction layer for SAP applications. Our objective is to:

  • Minimize the number of groups sent to each application.
  • Avoid exposing unrelated enterprise groups to applications.
  • Keep SAML assertions small and application-specific.
  • Centralize authorization mapping within IAS instead of requiring application-specific group filtering.

If IAS supports this capability, could you please advise which feature or configuration should be used (for example, application-specific attribute mappings, conditional transformations, or another recommended approach)?

entry.title: Re: How to set up the summary row function for summing ca...
entry.content:
@030169 ,

The Summary Row in SAP Analytics Cloud only aggregates measures using the aggregation defined in the model (such as Sum, Average, Count, etc.). If you're using a calculated measure, the total is recalculated at the aggregate level rather than summing the individual row results, which is standard SAC behavior. If you need the summary row to display the sum of all row values, define the field as a measure with Sum aggregation or move the calculation to the model or backend (Datasphere, HANA, or CDS View) so the value is calculated before it reaches the story. If you can share the calculation formula you're using, it will be easier to recommend the best approach.

entry.title: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code: Version 1.1 released
entry.content:
What happened since the last release?

First of all, thanks for the positive feedback regarding the 1.0.1 patch which added the much requested RFC + password support! Please keep providing feedback as it helps us to plan the next releases. A lot of users are asking us how they can provide feedback. We have recently added a new customer influence channel just for the VS Code extension. We recommend using that one.

Last month, ABAPConf 2026 took place in Mannheim. We want to highlight two talks:

  1. Tobias Hofmann provided public feedback regarding the extension and compiled a nice to-do list. I can report that a lot of these to-do items are already part of our internal to-do list. The talk can be found here.
  2. Anne Keller and I gave a talk on the overall ABAP IDE strategy. Of course, it touches the VS Code extension but also aims to provide a broader picture on how we want to support ABAP IDEs via a single technology stack in the future and shares some insights on how the ADT MCP server will evolve in the future. The talk can be found here.

 

What gets shipped with this release?

Usability improvements for system connection handling

After creating a new destination there was no visual feedback that the destination was created successfully. We added a notification pop-up with a button which directly adds it as a folder to your workspace.

We added a "Log on to Destination..." command. While it was possible to log on to a destination via a right-click menu in the explorer view there was no command available for it.

Now, you can easily log on to a destination using the keyboard via the new command.

A significant improvement of object type support

A lot of requested object types get enabled by this release. Here is the list of added object types:

Classic ABAP source code
  • Create and Manage Function Groups and Modules
  • Create and Manage ABAP Programs and Includes
  • Create and Manage Lock Objects
Data Dictionary
  • Create and Manage Database Tables
  • Create and Manage Structures
  • Create and Manage Type Groups
RESTful Application Programming Model
  • Creating Behavior Definition Extensions
  • Enabling more OData Binding Types in Service Binding
  • Create and Manage CDS Entity Buffers

New MCP tools

Unified diff tool for transports

The unified diff tool generates a git like diff for a given transport request. The tool enables token efficient analysis of the changes contained in a transport request. This enables AI agents to fulfill requests such as:

  • "Explain transport XYZ"
  • "Generate a description for transport XYZ documenting what got changed and why"
  • "How much impact does transport XYZ has on the system? How dangerous is this transport?"
Tools for ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC)

The ATC MCP tools give AI agents access to running the ATC checks for a given change. This enables AI agents to fulfill requests such as:

  • "Implement [XYZ]. Afterwards, run the ATC checks and fix the reported findings."
  • "Run ATC checks for object XYZ"

Additional UX improvements

We added a command which allows you to quickly open the SAP Help documentation.

From each occurrence of a CDS annotation in a CDS data definition or CDS metadata extension, you can now navigate to its CDS annotation definition. To navigate, press F3 on the relevant CDS annotation. This helps you to investigate and to understand the use of a CDS annotation.

For more information see the new "What's New" page.

 

Sidenote: first version of ABAP cleaner VS Code extension available

Lots of community members asked for ABAP cleaner support in VS Code. We are happy to announce that the first version of the ABAP cleaner extension is available, now!

 

What's next?

From now on, we will stop listing upcoming features directly in the release blogs. Instead we have cleaned up and refreshed our ABAP Cloud Roadmap page. The planned features are listed there.

Provide feedback and influence our product backlog!

We welcome your feedback! Feel free to use the comment section or create a customer influence request to let us know which features you are missing the most. Your input will help us prioritize the feature backlog for the next releases.

entry.title: Join the SAP Distributed Energy Resources Partner Collaboration Space
entry.content:
The energy transition is accelerating, giving rise to new business opportunities and challenges in the utility industry. These advancements in both operational technologies and market roles has made close ecosystem collaboration even more critical. As utilities, technology providers, and implementation partners work together to address the growing complexity of distributed energy resources, we believe that open communication and knowledge sharing are essential to delivering innovative and scalable solutions.

To support this collaboration, SAP is pleased to introduce the SAP Distributed Energy Resources (SAP DER) Partner Zone, a dedicated space designed to keep our partner ecosystem informed, engaged, and connected.

 

Target Audience for SAP DER Partner Zone

The SAP DER Partner Zone is built specifically for our partner ecosystem. Whether you are developing complementary solutions, implementing customer projects, or exploring new opportunities within the DER ecosystem, this platform is here to help you stay informed, connected, and aligned with the latest developments across SAP DER solution.

 

Why We Built the SAP DER Partner Zone

The SAP DER Partner Zone has been created to provide a single destination where partners can access relevant information required to implement our SAP DER solution and actively engage with the SAP DER team.

More importantly, this is not intended to be a one-way communication channel. SAP DER Partner Zone is aimed to become a collaborative environment where ideas can be shared, questions can be raised, and valuable feedback can help shape the future of the SAP DER solution.

What You’ll Find in the SAP DER Partner Zone

As SAP DER continues to evolve, the Partner Zone will continue to grow with new content and resources. Today, you can access the following:

Collaboration Space: A timeline-based space where you can mention your questions, ideas, and feedback. This also includes your questions asked during the enablement sessions and responses from SAP.

Enablement Material: Recorded video and textual content on the following topics:

  • Overview of SAP DER, measurement concept management component solution
  • Integration of SAP DER, measurement concept management component with 3rd Party systems
  • Extension and customization of SAP DER, measurement concept management component solution
  • Configuration of SAP DER, measurement concept management component solution
  • Relevant Admin and user guides for SAP DER, measurement concept management component
  • Introductory videos of SAP DER, measurement concept management component and its data model
  • Several demos showing exciting features and capabilities of SAP DER, measurement concept management component

Supporting Documents: Links to relevant help pages, news and updates on SAP DER solution.

Development Documents: Available documents on API packages and API guides.

 

How to join SAP DER Partner Zone?

SAP DER Partner Zone is a private space therefore explicit registration is required to access the content.

To request the access, please follow the steps below:

  • Open the SAP DER Partner Zone link
  • Sign in using your SAP ID, select Register and complete the registration process.
  • Enter the required information on the next page to register for your SAP ID.
  • After signing in with your SAP ID, you will be directed to the SAP DER Partner Zone access page.
  • Just enter a short description for your access (Optional) and click on “Request to Join“.
  • Your request will be reviewed by the SAP DER team.
  • Once approved, you will gain access to the SAP DER Partner Zone and its resources. 

 

Moving Forward – Building the Future Together

The challenges and opportunities within distributed energy resources continue to evolve rapidly, and we believe that with close collaboration with our partners, we can build a strong and scalable solution ecosystem to ensure a smooth energy transition.

This Partner Zone is intended to become a central hub for that collaboration, a place where information flows in both directions. While we’ll use this platform to share updates and insights, we also encourage you to contribute your own perspectives, ask questions, and participate in discussions.

Your expertise, experience, and feedback are invaluable in helping us develop solutions that address critical customer needs and support the evolving energy landscape.

Thank you for being part of the SAP DER partner ecosystem. We look forward to working together and continuing to drive innovation in the utilities market.

entry.title: Re: Material Inventory Values – Balance Summary Report Sh...
entry.content:
Good day @Mohit_Kumar20 

 

Thank you for your question

Your report does not show an overall negative physical inventory rather; it shows the inventory quantity and value spilt between two profit centers.

  • Profit center 100306: -273 EA and -139,982 INR
  • Profit center 100206: 807 EA and 540, 482 INR
  • Total: 534 EA and 400, 500 INR

The Material Inventory values – Balance Summary reports inventory by accounting dimensions such as company code, G/L account, plant, product, segment, and profit center. A negative quantity at one profit center can therefore exist even when the product’s total physical stock is positive.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/ed83861d4d8c4813b95a726a5303c401.html?locale=en-US

This frequently occurs when the product’s profit center assignment was changed after inventory transactions had already been posted. Earlier receipts may remain assigned to the previous profit center, while later goods issues or receipts are posted to the new profit center. The report then shows you an accounting spilt, not necessarily incorrect storage location stock.

Here are areas you should analyse:

In Material Inventory Values – Balance Summary, select the negative profile row and then go to Material Inventory Values – Lines Items.

Make sure you filter by product, plant, profit centers and reporting date which is 11.06.2026

Ensure you review the goods movements and journal entries that created the negative balance. The line item is intended for analysis inventory quantities and values down to individual postings.

You can compare the result with Check Stock overview to confirm the actual logistical stock quantity.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/bef78885c879469ba00724c138411a29.html?locale=en-US

 

There is no general correction programme that can simply overwrite your balance. You should not post a physical inventory adjustment merely to correct the report, because the total physical quantity is already positive.

If you intend to change the profit center as part of an approved organisational restructuring, then you can use the standard organisational change process. The profit center reorganization functionality creates transfer postings that offset values on the old profit center and assign them to the new profit center.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f0d042517a434458b171ff5867b18445/e3631a511c24406499741439154bdcac.html?locale=en-US

If you cannot implement a formal reorganization, or the negative balance cannot be explained by the line items, you can raise a case with the line-item export, product master profit center history, and relevant material and accounting documents.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: JSX Runtime for UI5: write your views as TSX, and tell us what you think
entry.content:
JSX Runtime for UI5: write your views as TSX, and tell us what you think

We've been experimenting with something and we'd like to put it in front of the community early: a JSX runtime for UI5. It lets you author UI5 views as .tsx files, with full per-control TypeScript typing, and it does so without a virtual DOM, and without a reconciler.

This is an incubation. It works, the showcase runs end-to-end, but the whole point of this post is to get it into your hands and hear back from you before we commit to more. If you write UI5 apps, we want your opinion.

What it actually does

A JSX element compiles to a plain constructor call. That's the entire idea:

import Button from "sap/m/Button";
import Page from "sap/m/Page";

export default class Main extends View {
    createContent() {
        return (
            <Page title="Hello">
                <Button text="Click me" press=".onPress" />
            </Page>
        );
    }
}

<Button text="Click me" /> becomes new Button({ text: "Click me" }). Nothing more magical than that. Babel emits the calls, the runtime hands them to UI5, and from there it's UI5 exactly as you already know it: metadata, applySettings, bindings, aggregations, events. There's no re-render loop, because a JSX expression is a constructor call, not a render description. Reactivity comes from UI5 bindings, same as in an XML view.

One thing to set expectations up front: this JSX describes the UI5 control tree, not the DOM. There's no native HTML. A <div> or <span> won't work, because a lowercase tag can't become a UI5 control. You compose controls (<Page>, <Button>, <VBox>, …), exactly as you do in an XML view, and UI5's renderers produce the actual markup. (An HTML-aware renderer is on the radar, but it's not here today.)

If you don't use TypeScript, plain .jsx works too. You still get the new Control(settings) calls, you just skip the author-time type checking.

Why we think it's worth a look

  • Per-control typing that we didn't hand-write. <Button> is typed from Button's own $ButtonSettings interface, which @openui5/types already publishes from each control's metadata. Wrong prop names, wrong value types, wrong event payloads. The TypeScript checker catches them. No code generation step in the project.
  • It's the control tree you already have. XML views and TSX views coexist in the same app. You can migrate one view at a time and keep everything runnable, or never migrate at all and just write new views in TSX.
  • No fork of UI5. The runtime doesn't patch or replace anything in the core. It glues Babel's output into UI5's existing settings model.
  • A small set of directives that read well. <Fragment> for grouping, <For> for bound aggregations, <If> for conditional rendering and falsy children ({flag && <X/>}) just work without crashing an aggregation.
  • A plugin SPI, if you want to extend it. Five extension points let libraries add their own directives and behaviors on top of the core without touching it. Everything the core itself does, the dot-handler events, <For>, <If>, is built on that same SPI, so nothing in the core is privileged. We ship a <Switch>/<Case>/<Default> sample plugin as a worked example.

Where to start

The fastest way to get a feel for it is the live showcase. It's a running UI5 app that doubles as the documentation, with runnable samples for every concept:

👉https://ui5-community.github.io/jsx-runtime/

The source, the setup guide, and the monorepo (the library, the showcase, and a minimal from-scratch reference app) are on GitHub:

👉https://github.com/ui5-community/jsx-runtime

If you already have an app with XML views, there's a step-by-step guide for converting them one at a time.

This is where you come in

We're calling this an incubation on purpose. Before we invest further, we want to know whether it solves a real problem for you, where it feels awkward, and what's missing. Concretely, we'd love to hear:

  • Would you write new views in TSX? Would you migrate existing ones?
  • Does the typing carry its weight, or is it more ceremony than value?
  • What breaks, or what did you expect to work and it didn't?
  • What would you need before you'd use this in a real project?

Please open an issue with anything: a bug, a rough edge, a "this is great," a "this is not for me and here's why":

👉https://github.com/ui5-community/jsx-runtime/issues

Honest feedback, including the critical kind, is exactly what makes an incubation worth doing. Thanks for taking a look.

entry.title: Clarification Request – Purchase Order Delivery Completed Indicator Behavior
entry.content:
Hi Team,

Please refer to QSA PO 1300004711. In this case, an inbound delivery has been created and the goods receipt is still awaited. However, the system allowed us to mark the PO line item as “Delivery Completed.”

We would like to confirm whether this is standard SAP behavior. Specifically, business users want to prevent this activity until the goods receipt is fully completed.

As per our understanding, the current system behavior is correct and logical, since the “Delivery Completed” indicator is intended to close PO line items where no further goods receipts are expected.

Could you kindly confirm this behavior and, if available, share any SAP documentation or notes that explain the design logic behind this functionality?

Thanks & Regards,

Pramod Kumar

entry.title: Re: Mapping and configuration for security deposit paid o...
entry.content:
In SAP RE-FX, security deposits are generally designed for deposits received from tenants. For a security deposit paid on a lease, you may need to configure it as a separate vendor/tenant-related payment or receivable/payable process and map the relevant accounts through FI integration.

The exact configuration depends on whether the deposit is paid to a landlord, vendor, or another business partner.

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports 2025 patch 1200 hangs during install
entry.content:
Often your AV and Firewall can block the installer because it is modifying your PC, you may need to temporarily disable them to get past the pause, there may be a pop-up waiting behind the progress you may not see or possibly on your second screen if you have one or it could be you don't have a second screen and the message has been sent to a non-second screen.

Hold the Shift and then hit the TAB key, and see if there are any screens showing permissions or some error etc. to possibly catch the error causing the pause.

Look in your AV logs to see if it blocked anything.

To remove upgrading issue using MSIExec.exe SAP Install team did the following:

1. Upgrade completely removes the last install, removing registry keys etc. before updating the new version

2. YOU MUST right click on the EXE and select Run as Administrator:

This gives the installer full permissions to remove/add the various dll's and registry entries.

Look here: C:\Users\YourUser name\AppData\Local\Temp\2026.07.21.05.30.58, date may be different

Open the latest install log, you should find a file called: setupengine.log

If there is one see if any errors are logged.

You may need to be patient, it can take a long time when upgrading

entry.title: Re: MCP Servers on SAP Integration Suite : Build Your First MCP Server from an API
entry.content:
Great article on MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite! I successfully deployed my MCP Server following your steps.

One question: in the section "Invoke the MCP Server Using an AI Client", you mention using Cline with PIR credentials but say the detailed steps are outside the scope of the blog.

Could you share how you configured Cline to connect to the MCP Server? I'm getting a Language model unavailable error and I'm not sure if it's related to the LLM provider configuration or the MCP authentication.

As a workaround I use Claude Code CLI — it calls the MCP server directly from a Node.js process, so it supports custom headers properly. With a pre-generated PIR token passed in ~/.claude.json, I can query S/4HANA Sales Orders via the MCP GW using natural language.

entry.title: Re: Vendor is not selected based on the Fixed vendor chec...
entry.content:
Hello @Pavan30 

As per note 3327584 - FAQ MRP Sourcing Guidance, 2.2.1 Fixed Vendor is not considered:

Within Source list it is possible to maintain a 'Fixed Vendor'. Idea is that within Procurement process this Vendor should be preferred, for example using transaction ME51n and using 'Source Determination' or button 'Assign Source of supply'. This flag and functionality is within MM-PUR but not part of PP-MRP.

...

To prefer the vendor, the source list flag 'Fix' (Fixed Source of Supply) was added as a SORT criteria for MRP, in case multiple sources are marked as relevant in source list (which is against SAP recommendations). Details are in SAP Note 2614920 Variant 2.

As per note 2614920 - Sourcing priority during MRP in S/4 HANA system, variant 2:

The following explanation shows step-by-step how system decides which source of supply should be picked.

  1. Source of supply assigned in quota.
  2. Source of supply for which a source list entry is maintained with MRP Planning <> ‘<space>’

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Route Determination for One-Time Customers in S/4HANA...
entry.content:
@Manish_12 If I get it correctly, you get sales order data from an external source without transportation zones. Then you enhance the data and calculate transportation zones based on ZIP codes. Then you create sales orders with calculated transportation zones.

You say that sales order creation doesn't determine routes with the transportation zones provided. But when you change sales orders and change transportation zones, then routes are determined correctly.

If my understanding is correct, then it is a bug in S/4 and needs to be reported to SAP support. Sales order logic should determine a route with the transportation zone provided during creation as well as during change.

entry.title: Find your next SAP Enterprise Support Academy live session
entry.content:
Whether you're looking for a quick expert webcast or a hands-on implementation workshop, the new Schedules for Upcoming Live Sessions page provides a single view of upcoming SAP Enterprise Support Academy learning opportunities. With more than 300 live sessions currently available for enrollment, you can easily browse upcoming sessions and filter them by session type, language, start date, and time zone:

Choose from two live learning formats:

  • Meet-the-Expert (MTE) – Live webcasts covering a range of topics and the support aspects of the latest SAP technologies. Learn directly from SAP experts and ask your questions during the live Q&A. Most one-time sessions are also available as recordings.
  • Expert-guided Implementations (EGIs) – Hands-on, multi-day remote workshops designed to help you apply what you learn directly in your own SAP environment. Each session follows a simple, practical approach: an SAP expert explains each activity step by step, you complete the activity in your own system, and guidance is available throughout.

Looking for sessions in your preferred language? In addition to English, you can find live sessions in Spanish, Portuguese, Chinese, and Japanese. Simply use the Language filter to discover the sessions that best suit you, including a growing selection of Spanish-language Expert-guided Implementations.

Session topics cover a broad range of SAP solutions and technologies, helping you build skills, adopt best practices, and achieve your business goals.

All live sessions are included with SAP Enterprise Support and SAP Enterprise Support, cloud editions as part of the Foundational Success Plan. To register, sign in to the SAP Learning site with your S-User and access SAP Enterprise Support Academy.

👉 Browse the new scheduling overview.

Still deciding which session to join? Subscribe to our monthly newsletter for curated highlights, news, and the latest learning and enablement opportunities from SAP Enterprise Support Academy.

entry.title: SAP BOP ACL (Availability Change Log )
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

We have a scenario in SAP BOP ACL in SAP S4 HANA aATP 2023 version. We selected a material/ Plant combination which had around 100 quantity stock. Then we further posted stock of 500 quantities in the same Material/Plant Combination. In our BOP Segment we only gave Plant (same plant for which we posted stock earlier) as a selection parameter and in Prioritizer we gave sort condition as Record creation date and the entry time. In BOP Variant header we gave Demand and Supply as the ACL parameters. 

Then we created a Sales order of 300 quantities which got confirmed as we have 600 quantity of Stock for the Material/Plant combination after stock posting as mentioned above. in the next step we DE confirmed the entire quantity manually in VA02 and the schedule lines was confirmed with 0 quantities. 

In the next step we executed the BOP manually to see what it does. Once the run was completed we found that BOP selected our material and confirmed it but it also took other Materials in its list and confirmed. 

But here the expectation was it should only select  our material for which stock was posted and supply got increased and the order was manually DE confirmed. As we have ACL selection on in our BOP variant (Demand/Supply as selection parameter).  But instead its selecting all other materials also in the BOP run. 

Can anyone help us to understand what we are missing here?

entry.title: Manage Service Entry Sheets App - Company Code, Plant and G/L Account columns not available
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and have observed a limitation in the Manage Service Entry Sheets app.

In the Settings (Table Personalization) of the Manage Service Entry Sheets app, the following fields are not available as selectable columns:

Company Code
Plant
G/L Account

Our customer operates across multiple Company Codes and Plants. Since these fields are not available in the list view, users are unable to easily identify, filter, or segregate Service Entry Sheets by Company Code or Plant.

Additionally, the G/L Account is only visible after opening an individual Service Entry Sheet and navigating to the line-item details. There is no option to display the G/L Account directly in the main list view.

In the customer's existing SAP ECC system, these fields are available in the list view, allowing users to review multiple Service Entry Sheets on a single screen without opening each document individually.

Steps to Reproduce

Open the Manage Service Entry Sheets app.
Navigate to the list of Service Entry Sheets.
Click Settings (Table Personalization).
Review the list of available columns.
Observe that Company Code, Plant, and G/L Account are not available as selectable columns.

Business Requirement

The customer processes a large volume of Service Entry Sheets across multiple Company Codes and Plants. Displaying these fields in the main list view would enable users to efficiently identify, filter, and monitor Service Entry Sheets without opening each document individually. The current behavior increases processing time and reduces operational efficiency.

Questions

Is this a standard limitation of the Manage Service Entry Sheets app in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?
Is there any standard configuration, adaptation, or key user extensibility option available to display Company Code, Plant, and G/L Account in the list view?
If this is a standard limitation, is there any SAP roadmap item or recommended alternative to fulfill this business requirement?

I have attached screenshots showing the available columns in the Settings menu for reference.

Thank you in advance for your guidance.

entry.title: unexplained errors in training customzing system KC2/100
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am currently using the training customizing KC2/100 system for the following certification:

SAP Certified Application Associate – Implementation Consultant – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting (C_S4CFI).

I am experiencing an issue with one of my tasks where error messages occur even though I am using the exact same settings and following the same steps each time.

What makes this issue difficult to understand is that the process sometimes works successfully with identical settings and input values, while at other times it results in an error message.

 

I have already verified that:

  • The configuration settings are identical.
  • The same master data and parameters are being used.
  • The process steps are executed in the same order.
  • No changes have been made to the system configuration.

In the app: Implementation Activities -> Define Tolerance Groups for G/L Accounts, i have to do a new entry with company code 1010, Tolerance Group 195 und Tolerances up to 5.00 EUR for Debit and Credit Posting. 

In the app "Manage G/L Account Master Data" a new G/L account "11002195" is created by copying another G/L account "11002000". A checkmark was set on the newly created account for 'open item management' because that’s the requirement.

Then in the app: "post general journal entries" two entries are created. One entry has a value of 1000 € and the other entry has a value of 995 €, so there's a difference of 5 €, which is needed for this task.

In the app "Clear G/L Accounts - Manual Clearing" the created account 11002195 gets cleared. But when I click on "post," I get the following error message: Balance in transaction currency EUR 5.00 is too large for automatic correction.  

However, I previously completed the same task with the identical settings, and the clearing was done then.

Best Regards,
Auron

entry.title: Explore SAP Service Cloud Version 2 APIs
entry.content:
In my work to explore how to build AI Agents, finding and understanding APIs used in SAP Service Cloud Version 2, is a large part of the work. While using 'inspection' in the browser is a great tool I wanted to have a better option without creating a larger application.

When I asked Claude, it suggested to work with 'HAR' files that can be downloaded from the browser. I then asked Claude to create a viewer of this file and organize the information in a more readable form.

The result was the below view:

The workflow is super simple. Let's say we want to analyze what API calls are made when opening the Contract Account View in SAP Service Cloud Version 2.

  1. Open the browser in inspection mode

  2. Navigate to a step just before opening the Contract Account View

  3. Clear the network tab for any calls made so far

  4. Open the Contract Account in SAP Service Cloud Version 2

  5. Save the HAR file (see screenshot for steps in the readme on GitHub)

After that just open the html viewer. At the top there is a 'choose file' option, and select the file you saved above. That is, it.

On the view you see the list of calls and the sequence of the calls.

If you select one of the calls it will show the payload for the call:

 

After a few iterations with Claude, we added a few more features to the viewer that could be helpful for other things:

  1. The original target was to get a list of the API calls made

  2. In addition, the payloads are added for the call. This makes it easy to view the actual data.

  3. At the left top you can select calls that fail and look at the payload for the error. In the attached example where we have an error - the payload directly gives you this information: "message": "No active communication configuration found.". That is pretty helpful, now I know to go an activate that.

 

I have uploaded the viewer on GitHub, Link to viewer.

To download the viewer: open the link above, click the Raw button, then use File > Save As in your browser to save the file as viewer.html. Save it anywhere convenient — your Desktop, a project folder, wherever you keep your HAR files. SINCE THIS IS CODE DELIVERED 'AS-IS', PLEASE USE AT YOUR OWN RISK AND VALIDATE BEFORE EXECUTING.

 

⚠ Important Notice Before You Start

HAR files contain sensitive data. When you export a HAR file from your browser, it captures everything — full request and response bodies, session cookies, authentication tokens, and any personal or business data returned by the APIs. Treat HAR files as confidential documents. Do not share them over unencrypted channels, do not attach them to emails or public tickets, and delete them when they are no longer needed.

JavaScript must be enabled. The viewer is an HTML file that runs JavaScript locally in your browser. Some corporate browser security policies restrict JavaScript execution for files opened from the local filesystem (file:// URLs). If the viewer opens but does not respond when you load a HAR file, your organization's security policy may be blocking it — check with your IT department before proceeding.

 

Maybe you can take this and add additional features that would make it even better for you.

Have a great week.

entry.title: Re: Internal Order planning in SAP S/4HANA with SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content: Yes, the IOs created in S/4HANA are real IOs. You can use status management of an IO to allow actual postings in S/4HANA to it. The status management of the IO in S/4HANA only influences the allowed transactions in S/4HANA.

entry.title: Trapped in Transformation OR The Uncomfortable Truth of the EA in an S/4HANA Transformation
entry.content:
AI generated

 

Imagine this: a medium-sized fashion company—agile, successful, but with an IT landscape that has grown organically over the years… or, put differently: wildly.

The heart of its process’s beats in three different rhythms—three metronomes, none synchronized: Fxxxxx for retail, Ixxxxx for wholesale, and Nxxxxxxx for financial accounting & controlling. This kind of fragmentation is expensive, inefficient, and a serious brake on digital transformation. The logical consequence: a comprehensive transformation to SAP S/4HANA for fashion and retail.

A mammoth project. And right in the middle of it: the Enterprise Architect (EA).

The textbook role: the EA as a strategic enabler

The classic picture of the Enterprise Architect revolves around technology: systems, platforms, integrations, interfaces. They think in terms of architectural layers, evaluate tools, and safeguard the technical landscape. That matters—but it isn’t enough.

An EA who truly makes an impact doesn’t think in systems. They think in the business. They understand how processes work, where decisions are made, what the strategic goals are—and which technological choices steer the company in a certain direction. Every architecture decision is therefore also a business decision: it defines what becomes possible and what doesn’t, what costs and what saves, what scales and what slows things down.

An EA who speaks only in IT categories will be tolerated. An EA who thinks like a business leader and actively steers through decisions will be heard—and needed.

Transformation as a permanent state

In the past, transformation was a clearly defined project with a beginning, a middle, and an end. Today, it’s a continuous operating mode—and it devours resources. The longer a transformation runs, the more internal employees get pulled into roles that aren’t really theirs. The project manager becomes the test coordinator, the executive assistant becomes the change manager, the architect becomes the data migration lead.

This kind of role drift isn’t the exception—it’s the pattern. It’s reinforced by a company spirit that not only tolerates taking on new roles but actively encourages it. Anyone willing to step outside their comfort zone isn’t punished in a transformation—they’re needed.

Companies systematically underestimate how much internal know-how a transformation consumes—and how many people inevitably have to outgrow themselves along the way. That creates pressure, but it also creates an opportunity: if you perform well in an unfamiliar role, you come back stronger. You grow beyond yourself—usually when you have no other choice.

And the EA? In this environment, they must guarantee stability and enable change—while they themselves have long since ended up in a role that was never part of the plan.

Trapped between aspiration and reality

The classic EA aspiration

The traditional image of the Enterprise Architect is that of a strategic designer: they design target architectures, define standards, create transparency across the enterprise landscape, and derive recommendations for action. Frameworks like TOGAF provide structured approaches for this.

The transformative reality

In practice, however, the EA often faces a very different reality:

  • Target architectures become outdated before they’re fully implemented
  • Decisions are made decentralized—without EA involvement
  • Technical debt grows faster than it can be reduced
  • Stakeholders decide by time and budget—while architectural sustainability politely waits outside the meeting invite.
  • Enterprise architecture documentation lags reality

The EA ends up trapped between what should be—and what actually is.

What this means for the EA role

1) From planner to enabler

The EA can no longer act primarily as a central planner. Instead, they must become an enabler and coach—someone who empowers decentralized teams to make architecturally sound decisions without having to personally make every decision.

“Architecture is not about control; it's about enabling good decisions at scale.”

2) From static models to a living landscape

Instead of complex, one-off architecture models, what’s needed are lightweight artifacts that are maintained continuously: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs), living wikis, automatically generated landscape maps. The model has to be able to breathe.

3) From framework dogmatism to pragmatism

TOGAF phases and Zachman cells have their value—but in a world of continuous transformation, EA methods must be applied situationally and pragmatically. No framework fits every situation unchanged.

4) From silo guardian to bridge builder

The EA must actively build bridges: between IT and business, between strategic planning and operational execution, between innovation and governance. Stay in your silo—and you lose relevance.

What the EA needs to avoid freezing up

Technical competence still matters

Even as the EA becomes more strategic, they must understand what’s happening technically—AI, cloud-native architectures, API ecosystems, data platforms. Credibility comes from competence, not from titles.

Communication is a core skill

The best architecture plan is useless if no one understands it. EAs must learn to explain complex things simply—at board level and in engineering teams alike.

Resilience and ambiguity tolerance

Transformation means uncertainty. No complete picture, no final answers—just the ability to remain effective anyway and move forward iteratively.

Networking and visibility

Enterprise architecture doesn’t happen in a back room. The EA must be visible, present, and connected—across projects, committees, and communities of practice. If you’re not heard, you’ll be bypassed.

The “off script” role: the EA as Data Migration Lead

No topic in S/4HANA projects is underestimated as consistently as data migration. It’s not a technical side quest—it’s the foundation the entire new world stands on. And that’s exactly why it lands with the EA: because it’s critical, messy, and “kind of technical.” The implicit assumption being, of course, that the EA can simply absorb a full-scale data migration on top of their existing responsibilities—no additional headcount, no external support, no problem. Spoiler: no. For a while, he'll have to unlearn what has become second nature.

By doing so, the EA deliberately steps out of the classic role. They’re no longer the architect at the drawing board, but the Data Migration Lead—archaeologist, diplomat, and crisis manager all in one. This role shift isn’t a failure. It’s a strategic investment in a skill set that most EAs only ever talk about.

What challenges the EA here

The migration topics are multifaceted—and each is its own discipline:

  • Business Partner: customers from Fxxxxx, Ixxxxx, and Nxxxxxxx must be consolidated into a central SAP Business Partner—without duplicates, with clear role logic (customer, supplier, prospect) and consistent master data.
  • Article & material master: color codes, product groups, season flags, and size logic from the source systems must be mapped to a unified S/4HANA data model. Every wrong mapping hits inventory, logistics, and reporting immediately.
  • Open orders: purchase orders and sales orders that span the go-live cutover date must be migrated completely and correctly—no order can be lost.
  • Inventory: the hardest exercise. Physical stock and migrated values must match exactly. Phantom stock and unexplained variances always show up precisely when you need them least. And the quality bar is brutal: inventory values worth several million euros are transferred into the new system. Any deviation hits the balance sheet—and inevitably triggers the auditor. They’ll ask politely—once. After that, the tone tends to change.

What the EA gains from it

This role shift isn’t a detour—it’s an investment in a competence profile no textbook can teach:

  • Deep business understanding: if you know how data really flows, you understand the company at a level no org chart or process model can capture.
  • Stakeholder trust: the EA who has worked in the engine room is taken seriously by business and leadership alike—they’ve proven they can deliver.
  • Architectural credibility: post-migration decisions on data governance, master data management, and system integration are no longer theory—they’re lived experience.
  • Career relevance: data-migration expertise is scarce and in demand. Anyone who has led an S/4HANA migration becomes a highly relevant leader for future transformations—internally and externally.
  • A foundation for real refactoring: after go-live, the EA knows exactly where the technical debt is, which compromises were made, and where cleanup must start. That makes them the best architect for the next phase.

LeanIX: the quiet hero nobody misses—until it’s gone

If you’ve lived through an S/4HANA transformation, you know this moment: someone asks, “Which systems are actually connected to Nxxxxxxx?” or “Who is the business owner of the integration interface to Fxxxxx?” And suddenly everyone looks around.

That’s when LeanIX’s relevance goes through the roof.

LeanIX is the EA’s central tool for application portfolio management and documenting the IT landscape. In calm times, many see it as a nice-to-have—a maintenance tool you “should update at some point.” Keeping LeanIX current when everything is quiet? “We’ll do it after the project.” The project lasts five years. Welcome to the vicious cycle. In a transformation, it turns into critical infrastructure.

Why LeanIX becomes non-negotiable in transformation

  • Make dependencies visible: which applications depend on the legacy systems being decommissioned? Which interfaces must be migrated—and which can be shut down? Without maintained LeanIX, these are assumptions, not facts.
  • Steer the coexistence phase: while old and new systems run in parallel, LeanIX can be the only source that keeps an overview of the landscape state—if it’s kept up to date.
  • Justify migration decisions: which application gets replaced, which stays? Which is technically outdated, which still has strategic relevance? LeanIX provides the data basis for those decisions.
  • Communicate with stakeholders: a maintained LeanIX portfolio replaces endless PowerPoint decks. It makes transformation progress visible—for management and project teams alike.

The bitter paradox

LeanIX is often valued only when the data is missing or out of date: when, during the cutover crunch, nobody can say which system is still writing data into Nxxxxxxx. When the interface documentation dates back to 2016—and reality has long since moved on.

The EA is often the only person who knows why a decision was made years ago—and is politely blamed for it today. That knowledge lives in no ticket, no Confluence page, and no LeanIX record. It sits in their head. If you don’t trust the EA, you’re not really trusting the long-term sustainability of your own IT landscape.

The EA who maintained LeanIX consistently before the transformation is the calmest person in the room in that moment. The EA who didn’t is the one who spends the next weeks reconstructing the landscape through interviews and manual analysis—under time pressure.

Good LeanIX maintenance isn’t bureaucracy. It’s the insurance you never miss—until you need it.

Transformation is also a chance to establish LeanIX permanently: not as “the EA team’s job”, but as a living tool owned by the entire IT organization.

Conclusion: more than an architect

Anyone who lives through this kind of transformation doesn’t come back the same. The EA has worked in the engine room as a Data Migration Lead, defended LeanIX under pressure, mediated across business units, and filled roles that were never in their job description. They outgrew themselves—usually when they had no other choice.

The most valuable skills they take away don’t initially sound like enterprise architecture: data quality, stakeholder diplomacy, operational crisis-readiness, systems thinking under pressure. But that’s exactly what separates a great EA from a good one.

These capabilities aren’t side activities—they’re the multiplier for everything the EA wants to deliver strategically. If you don’t know how data really flows, you design architectures in a vacuum. If you don’t have credibility in day-to-day operations, you won’t be heard strategically.

Your target architecture is beautiful. But reality will still ruin it—slowly, over time and within budget. And the project even has the board’s full support for these unsustainable decisions.

And LeanIX? Next time, they’ll update it first—not because someone demands it, but because they know what happens when nobody does.

The transformation wasn’t a detour. It was the shortest path to becoming a better architect.

In the end, nobody got exactly what they imagined. Everyone still emerged stronger—but everyone walks out with something rare: the shared credibility of having steered the company through the messy part — together, and often well outside their job descriptions.

That's transformation!

entry.title: Output/Form Determination Logic for the Exempt Purchase Order
entry.content:
Problem description

We have implemented the BAdI YY1_IVA_ORDEN_COMPRA to calculate and display the total values (net + VAT) on the Exempt Purchase Order form. We have detected that this logic is only executed once the document is already in released status. While the PO is in pending status (not yet released), the BAdI is not called, so the form only shows the net value and omits VAT and the total.

Current behavior

  • Pending / not released PO: the BAdI is not executed. The Total field remains blank or shows only the net value.
  • Released PO: the BAdI is executed and the totals are calculated and displayed correctly.

Expected behavior

That the form logic (BAdI) be executed consistently when the document is generated, across all statuses of the PO lifecycle, so that the calculation and display of the total are always correct regardless of whether the order is pending or released.

Steps to reproduce

  1. Create an Exempt Purchase Order and leave it in pending status (not released).
  2. Generate the print preview of the PO form.
  3. Observe that the Total field remains blank or shows only the net value.
  4. Release the PO and generate the form again: the total is displayed correctly.

Question

Is this the standard behavior of the output determination / form? At which point in the document lifecycle is the output BAdI invoked, and how can we ensure that the form logic is also executed while the PO is pending?

We remain available for any further information you may require (PO example, screenshots, and transport data).

entry.title: Re: Purchasing info record not updated after creation of PO
entry.content:
Dear @Ian2345,

Yes, this is expected system behavior

The Info Update indicator sets the purchase order as the last reference document in the purchasing info record and updates the order price history. However, it does not overwrite an existing valid price condition in the PIR

The official job template is Determine and Update the Price of Purchasing Info Record. Where a valid PIR condition exists this job does not replace that condition with a price manually entered in the purchase order. The job is used to synchronize the PIR price and validity data after the underlying condition value or validity period has changed

Therefore, if a valid PIR condition still contains the old price, that condition continues to determine the price in subsequent purchase orders. To use the new price consistently, update the relevant Gross Price condition directly in Manage Purchasing Info Records, ensuring that it is valid for the purchase order pricing date and maintained at the correct purchasing organization or plant level.

If no valid PIR condition exists, Create Purchase Order Advanced can determine the price from the last purchase order referenced in the info record, even where a price is entered manually in the new PO. This fallback behavior is application dependent Manage Purchase Orders does not retrieve the last PO price in the same way

More details below:

SAP KBA 3704795 – Price Is Not Updated in Purchasing Info Record Condition

SAP KBA 3589148 – How to Stop Price in Purchase Order Being Determined from Last Purchase Order of Info Record

SAP KBA 3620183 – Button “Info Update” in Purchasing Info Record App Doesn’t Work

SAP KBA 2827837 – How Is Last PO Price Picked Up in Purchase Order

SAP Help – Manage Purchasing Info Records

SAP Help – Schedule Update of Price for Purchasing Documents

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Data Cloud Seamless Planning – Data Retr...
entry.content:
Hi @DheerajHJ,

I'm not an expert from BDC, SAC, or DSP side, I'm responsible for the IFP business content available in SAC. 

In traditional SAC planning, planning model fact data is stored inside SAC itself. With BDC Seamless Planning, when you deploy a model to a Datasphere Space:

  • Planning logic, calculations, Data Actions, version management → remain entirely in SAC
  • Fact data and public dimension tables → are physically stored in SAP Datasphere 
  • SAC writes directly into that Datasphere table on every planning operation — no separate replication step

SAC is the planning UI and calculation engine. Datasphere is the persistence layer.

The export job is working as before, the SAC data acquisition framework (which includes the Plan Data Import / Export Job that writes to ACDOCP) is not changed by Seamless Planning. SAC exposes the model data via its export API regardless of where the model is stored. No additional configuration is needed specifically for the write-back.

Find below resources we provided for the IFP business content and the write-back of data into S/4HANA and S/4HANA Cloud

  • Exporting Data from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • Exporting Data from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Getting Started with SAP Analytics Cloud 

I found additional resources which might be helpful

  • SAP Community — Seamless planning integration between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere - product FAQ (SAP-authored): https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/seamless-planning-integration-between-sap-analytics-cloud-and-sap/ba-p/13877679

  • SAP Community — Understanding Seamless Planning (SAP-authored): https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/understanding-seamless-planning/ba-p/14273093

  • SAP Community — Migrating to Seamless Planning in SAP Analytics Cloud: Steps, Pitfalls, and Workarounds (SAP-authored): https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/migrating-to-seamless-planning-in-sap-analytics-cloud-steps-pitfalls-and/ba-p/14338388

Kind regards,
Susanne

entry.title: Re: Reading the Values of the Data Fields of Comconent Co...
entry.content:
Hi Puspita,

You don´t need extra Batch or Inventory data fields to capture component genealogy or batch traceability. In your initial screenshots, I also see you added custom fields for posting date, user id, component, storage location, etc, which are all already captured by the standard component consumption process.

DM handles batch managed components automatically, and will a require batch / inventory selection in those cases. If the component is not batch managed, then it´s not required, you don´t need to build that logic.

The 'Batch Number' field in 'Consume Component' popup is auto filled if a 'Planned Batch' was defined in ERP. In any case, if a 'Planned Batch' was defined or not, you can always click to browse 'Batch Number' field and select a specific Batch / Inventory ID available in DM as Floor Stock, see Transfer of Floor Stock Data at https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-digital-manufacturing/integration-guide/transfer-of-floor-stock-data for details on how to sync stock from ERP to DM.

It seems to me you might be trying to rebuild standard functionalities already in place. So, I suggest you go through DM learnings at https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/applying-sap-digital-manufacturing and look for stock / consumption details.

Hope it helps! Br, Manoel

entry.title: Re: I have a requirement where I can check if Attachment...
entry.content:
Dear @debangshusett,

No. In Public Cloud the attachment cannot currently be made technically mandatory in the standard Create Supplier Invoice application. The BAdI MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD - Check Invoice Data can block posting based on supplier invoice header and item data. The BAdI also provides action-specific hooks such as Post, Park, Hold and Save as Completed, so a custom validation can be targeted precisely at the posting step. However the attachment list or attachment count is not available in the BAdI interface. Therefore, it cannot check whether a file has been uploaded, either for all invoices or selectively for document types such as RE or KR

The attachment-related BAdI MRM_ATTACH_TO_READ_ONLY_CLOUD has a different purpose. It controls the creation or deletion of attachments for invoices that have already been parked or posted and cannot be used as a pre-posting validation

My proposals/workarounds are:

  • Create a custom checkbox such as Attachment Confirmed and validate it in MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD. This blocks posting if the checkbox is not selected but it does not technically verify that a file exists
  • Use Upload Supplier Invoices (F2452) as the controlled invoice-entry process. The uploaded document is automatically linked to the created invoice draft
  • Use the Parked as Complete workflow and require the approver to verify the attachment before posting. This must be supported by appropriate authorizations to prevent users from bypassing the workflow
  • For strict technical enforcement, implement a controlled SAP BTP or external invoice-intake solution that requires an attachment before submitting the invoice through the supplier invoice APIs

Summary:

The standard app and available BAdIs cannot validate the actual presence of an attachment before posting. Process controls are possible but a BTP or external intake solution is required for a hard technical attachment requirement

More details below:

SAP Help – Business Add-Ins for Invoice Processing

SAP Help – Upload Supplier Invoices

SAP Help – Workflow for Parked Invoice as Complete

SAP Help – Supplier Invoice – Create (A2A, Inbound, Asynchronous)

SAP Community – Supplier Invoice: Make Attachments Mandatory?

IMPORTANT: If the standard options do not meet the business requirement, an improvement request can be submitted through SAP Customer Influence, requesting that attachment presence be available for pre-posting validation or configurable as mandatory based on the supplier invoice document type

SAP Customer Influence – Submit an Improvement Request

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Chart of Accounts strategy for multi-country group implementation — which approach is recommended?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

We are implementing SAP FI for a group headquartered in Country A (HQ), with 3 Company Codes located in:

  • Country B 
  • Country C 
  • Country D 

Each subsidiary operates under a different local accounting standard. We are trying to decide on the right Chart of Accounts (COA) architecture and would appreciate feedback from consultants who have implemented similar multi-country scenarios.

We have identified 4 possible approaches:

  1. Single unified Operational COA for the whole group (no Country COA) simple, but doesn't natively support each country's local legal/fiscal reporting requirements.
  2. Local Operational COA per country + Group COA for consolidation (using the Alternative Account Number field)  each Company Code posts directly in its local GAAP, and the Group COA handles consolidation at HQ level.
  3. Unified Operational COA for daily group reporting + local Country COA per subsidiary for legal/fiscal compliance daily postings use the group-harmonized COA, and a Country Specific COA (mapped via Alternative Account Number) is used for statutory/tax reporting.
  4. All three COA types simultaneously (Operational + Group + Country) maximum coverage but heavier configuration and maintenance.

Our questions:

  • Which of these architectures is most commonly recommended for a group with 3+ subsidiaries under different local GAAPs?
  • In practice, does the end user post a single journal entry, with SAP handling the account mapping automatically via the Alternative Account Number field (OB13/OB62 configuration)? Or are there cases requiring separate postings?
  • Are there real-world limitations or pitfalls with Approach 2 or 3 when it comes to VAT reporting, financial statement versions (FSV), or statutory audit file requirements?
  • Any SAP Notes, Best Practices documentation, or real implementation experience you could share would be very helpful.

Thank you in advance for your insights!
SAP S/4HANA Finance  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Finance  SAP S/4HANA Finance for group reporting

entry.title: The Octopus Brain
entry.content:
An octopus has around 500 million neurons in a massive nervous system. Where humans concentrate most neurons in the head, an octopus does the opposite. About two-thirds live in the arms themselves — roughly 40 million per arm — forming eight semi-autonomous mini-brains. Each arm tastes, touches, and makes motor decisions on its own. A central brain coordinates.
That's the architecture I ended up building — accidentally, then deliberately — for the way Claude works with my knowledge. Hence: Project Octopus. Here's what broke, what fixed it, and a prompt you can borrow to try the same pattern on your own knowledge bases.

The problem: four brains, no conversation

The whole thing started after I worked through Ken Lomax's wonderful workshop, My Second Brain (you find similar content on Youtube) — the idea that you have your own wiki which Claude Code builds from a folder with raw files. The idea landed, and I got to work creating brains one at a time.

First I had just the one that helps me with organizing my tool knowledge, mostly in the unfathomable depths of ABAP documentation. Realizing that I cannot have one large brain for all my needs, I started to create other ones, to avoid dilution of knowledge. I created another one for the new field of code-based agents, and one for personal development which became a pretty cool career advisor as we are currently preparing our Q3 SAP talks. And another one for API and Business Object documentation.

Keeping them separate was intentional. A single mega-brain collapses under its own weight, mixing goal-setting advice with ABAP transaction quirks.

The trade-off: Claude only knew about the individual ones when you worked in the corresponding project folder. Sit inside one brain and drift into a topic that belongs to another, and Claude has no way of knowing the other brain even exists. The answer comes from raw model knowledge — plausible, generic, and sometimes wrong.

The fix: a coordinating brain

The solution turned out to be smaller than expected. Not a fifth brain, but a compact registry — one table in the global CLAUDE.md that every Claude session loads automatically. Each row names a brain, its path, its scope, and the topics that trigger a consult. Two rules follow: read the matching brain's index before answering, and reconcile across brains when a topic straddles two of them.

That's it. No new folders, no orchestration layer, no third-party tooling. Just a lookup table that lives where Claude already looks.

The octopus metaphor writes itself. The four brains are the arms — specialized, autonomous, each holding its own hard-won knowledge. The registry is the central brain, unimpressive on paper, doing the one thing central brains do well: routing signals to the arm that already knows the answer.

 

Try it yourself

If you have two or more brains and want the same routing setup, drop the following into a Claude session and let it do the discovery and wiring for you.

Note: Your brain folders might not be called `mybrain<Name>` — adapt the naming in the prompt below to match your own convention.

Here's the prompt:

I have multiple "second brain" folders on my machine — each is a folder named `mybrain<Something>` following the LLM-Wiki pattern, with `raw/`, `wiki/`, and a `CLAUDE.md` at the root. Right now Claude only sees whichever brain I happen to be sitting in, so knowledge in the other brains stays invisible for the session. Fix that. Steps:

1. **Find all my brains.** Search my user home directory and its subfolders (including any OneDrive folders) for directories matching `mybrain*` that contain a `CLAUDE.md` at their root. Skip `.zip` archives, empty folders, and staging folders that lack the full brain structure.

2. **Read each brain's `CLAUDE.md`** to pull out its purpose and scope. Do not modify anything inside the brain folders.

3. **Locate my global Claude config** at `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Windows: `C:\Users\<me>\.claude\CLAUDE.md`). If it does not exist, stop and tell me before creating anything.

4. **Prepare a new section** titled `## Personal Knowledge Bases (mybrain* wikis)` containing:
- A one-line introduction to the routing pattern.
- A markdown table with one row per brain, columns: **Brain**, **Path**, **Scope**, **Consult when**. Derive "Consult when" from each brain's own scope, pulling out two or three concrete topic keywords.
- A short workflow: read the matching brain's `wiki/index.md` first, cite pages, reconcile across brains when a topic spans two of them, fall back to model knowledge only when no brain covers the topic.
- A rule that each brain's `raw/` folder is read-only.

5. **Show me the preview** and ask me to confirm before writing the section into the global CLAUDE.md. Preserve everything already in that file — append or splice in cleanly, do not overwrite unrelated sections.

6. **After writing**, tell me how to verify: open a new Claude session, ask a question that clearly belongs to one specific brain from any folder on disk, and confirm Claude routes to that brain's wiki before answering.

Do not create any new brain folders. Do not modify anything inside the existing brains. Only edit the global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`.

Closing thoughts

That's the whole pattern: four arms of specialized knowledge, one small table in the middle, no orchestration layer. Total wiring cost was one section in a markdown file, and Claude now routes across brains without me having to remember which folder I'm sitting in.

If you're running multiple wikis and want the same routing, the prompt above will set it up for you. And if you've built a different way of stitching separate knowledge bases together, drop it in the comments — I'd like to see what other shapes this pattern can take.

entry.title: Re: Problem with Product SOA message not triggered during...
entry.content:
Hi Deepali,

Since you've already debugged CL_MDC_MODEL_MAT=>REPLICATE_MATERIAL, it looks like the issue occurs before the DRF API is called. If MATNR is blank in MARA_SRC_STAT, <ls_mat_drf_keys>-bo_keys will remain initial, so CL_DRF_MEMBERSHIP_API=>SEND_BO_INSTANCE_MULTI won't be executed.

I would suggest checking where LT_MARA_SRC is populated and whether MATNR is already blank at that stage. If it is, the problem is likely in the material selection or key preparation rather than in the DRF framework itself.

It would also be worth verifying the replication model and filters to make sure the material is included for replication.

Could you let us know whether MATNR is already blank before entering REPLICATE_MATERIAL, or does it become blank inside the method?

entry.title: Re: SQL Anywhere 17: How can DATABASE SIZE be used with a...
entry.content: I am not aware of any mechanism to size the database created during a dbunload operation.  Although, I believe that a database created by dbunload does get sized based on the source database - but it is conservative and would not reflect that actual size of the end database.  It also would not account for any future growth which I think the formula posted is intended to account for.

entry.title: ‌🇳🇴‌ User Group Norway - Conference 2026
entry.content:
We are inviting you to attend the User Group Norway Conference 2026, the annual gathering of the Norwegian SAP user community.

The conference brings together SAP customers, partners, experts, and SAP representatives to exchange experiences, discuss current developments, and explore the future of business transformation with SAP.

Participants can expect customer presentations, expert sessions, and networking opportunities covering key topics such as cloud transformation, AI, data, and localization requirements.

We look forward to connecting with the Norwegian SAP community and engaging in meaningful discussions throughout the event.

Find more information about the conference and get your ticket HERE. 

If you want to connect with SAP users and experts to exchange on Localization topics for Norway, join the regional collaboration groups:

  • Norway - Enterprise Resource Planning Localization
  • Norway — Human Capital Management Localization.

entry.title: Re: Sending APIM Alerts made easy using SAP CPI
entry.content: I’m not receiving the error_description field in the OData V4 API response. Please let me know if there are any additional steps I need to take beyond the ones listed above.

entry.title: Re: France: Register test participant ID with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition
entry.content:
@AdityaPusalkar It's correct that for 0225, the default destination is a mandatory step to be set up in the Manage Routing Rules as described in SAP Help. It's not available in the Create Participant screen.

You can set up additional rules for routing the incoming invoices, but at least 1 default destination is mandatory.

entry.title: ‌🇫🇮‌ SAP Finug Autumn Seminar
entry.content:
Join SAP Finug Syysseminaari 2026, the largest SAP Finug event of the year. The seminar brings together SAP customers, partners, and industry professionals for a day of knowledge sharing, practical insights, and networking.

Hear the latest SAP customer stories, expert presentations, and keynote sessions on topics shaping the SAP ecosystem. Connect with peers and SAP experts, explore an extensive partner exhibition, and exchange ideas with members of the Finnish SAP community.

The event also includes refreshments, networking opportunities, and an evening programme.

Find more information and register here.

If you want to connect with SAP users and experts to exchange on Localization topics for Finland, join the Finland - Human Capital Management Localization regional collaboration group.
